jokes.text: Remember when you asked to see my family tree? No.
jokes.setup: Professor claims someone asked to see his family tree
jokes.punchline: Immediate flat denial that anyone asked
jokes.explanation: Classic character moment - Professor's self-importance immediately deflated. Clean setup/punchline with perfect economy.

jokes.text: inventor of television, and as a bonus, childhood obesity
jokes.setup: Praising ancestor as inventor of television
jokes.punchline: Casually adding childhood obesity as a 'bonus' achievement
jokes.explanation: Dark twist on praising technological progress. The casual tone makes the subversive observation funnier.

jokes.text: Where is he, blasted?!
jokes.setup: Professor showing family tree of colorblindness test inventor
jokes.punchline: Professor can't see him due to his own colorblindness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual joke - the irony of not being able to see the colorblindness test inventor because of colorblindness. Perfect character integration.

jokes.text: No wormy fruit on this tree.
jokes.punchline: Sets up for immediate contradiction about Fry
jokes.explanation: Solid setup for the Fry insult that follows. The metaphor is competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: He's way over here on this filthy branch riddled with fungus and dung beetles.
jokes.setup: Dismissing Fry as 'not a true Farnsworth'
jokes.punchline: Elaborate visual description of how disgusting Fry's branch is
jokes.explanation: Professor's contempt for Fry expressed through vivid imagery. The specificity of 'fungus and dung beetles' elevates it.

jokes.text: What's with the 17 dung beetles? Well, it's 6:00.
jokes.setup: Asking about specific number of dung beetles on family tree
jokes.punchline: Professor treats it like normal work schedule
jokes.explanation: Surreal logic treating dung beetle activity like a work day. The absurdity works but isn't exceptional.

jokes.text: Time to go clubbing! Baby seals, here I come.
jokes.punchline: Reveals he means literally clubbing baby seals
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection joke with dark twist. Very characteristic of Bender's violent tendencies presented casually.

jokes.text: I need the extra money to buy coffee so I can stay awake for my night job.
jokes.setup: Fry explaining why he needs his night job
jokes.punchline: Circular logic - needs job to buy coffee to stay awake for the job
jokes.explanation: Clever circular logic joke that captures the absurdity of working just to afford to work. Classic Fry stupidity.

jokes.text: You've got a surprising amount of algae in your beard, Mr. President.
jokes.punchline: Casual observation about algae in presidential head's beard
jokes.explanation: Mild visual gag about the absurdity of maintaining preserved heads. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Oh! Lay off my trademark mole!
jokes.punchline: President protective of his distinctive mole
jokes.explanation: Minor character joke about vanity even in death. Serviceable but not memorable.

jokes.text: Fry. Whatever.
jokes.punchline: Lars's complete dismissiveness
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Lars's indifference to Fry. Simple but effective.

jokes.text: no head bowling, and no parties, or it'll be your ass in a jar
jokes.punchline: Threat plays on the jar theme of the Head Museum
jokes.explanation: Clever threat that plays on the museum's theme. The pun works because it fits the world.

jokes.text: Man, this place is a snooze.
jokes.setup: Bender at museum full of historical figures
jokes.punchline: Finding history boring despite having access to actual historical figures
jokes.explanation: Basic irony but doesn't really land hard. Bender's character but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: You're on, skinny.
jokes.setup: Bender challenging Taft to pie-eating contest
jokes.punchline: Famously obese president calling robot 'skinny'
jokes.explanation: Good character joke using Taft's historical reputation. The irony of calling a robot skinny lands well.

jokes.text: Come on, you pansy! I'm thirsty! I want me some Tennessee loopy juice!
jokes.punchline: Period-appropriate slang for alcohol
jokes.explanation: Amusing period language that sounds authentic while being silly. 'Loopy juice' is a nice touch.

jokes.text: And I want loose women! The kind that aren't afraid to show a little ankle. Or elbow.
jokes.punchline: Escalating to extremely modest body parts for the era
jokes.explanation: Great escalation joke playing on historical modesty. The elbow addition makes it funnier by being even more absurd.

jokes.text: Oh, boo-hoo, I'll lose my minimum wage job.
jokes.setup: Fry worried about losing his job
jokes.punchline: Bender's sarcastic dismissal of the stakes
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender dismissiveness. In character but not particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: You're mean, rutherford b. Hayes!
jokes.punchline: Using full presidential name as insult
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from using formal presidential name in casual insult. The specificity helps but it's not exceptional.

jokes.text: We have nothing to fear but running out of beer.
jokes.punchline: Changing 'fear itself' to 'running out of beer'
jokes.explanation: Solid parody of famous quote. Structure is perfect but the concept is fairly obvious.

jokes.text: So, you grow hemp. Yes. And, uh, you do what with it? All manner of things. Manufacture paper, fabric, rope... Oh. Well, nice talking to you.
jokes.setup: Someone interested in Washington's hemp growing
jokes.punchline: Disappointment when it's actually for legitimate uses
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection playing on modern assumptions about hemp. Washington's innocence makes the disappointment funny.

jokes.text: Why, I used to smoke about four feet of rope a day. Let me give you my pager number.
jokes.punchline: Misunderstanding leads to offering pager number
jokes.explanation: Washington's innocent misunderstanding of the hemp interest is amusing. The pager anachronism adds to it.

jokes.text: I asked you to leave me alone.
jokes.setup: Professor trying to show Reagan his family tree
jokes.punchline: Reagan's blunt rejection of the conversation
jokes.explanation: Simple deflation of Professor's enthusiasm. Effective but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Farnsworth?! That name is a stain on American history.
jokes.punchline: Reagan's dramatic denouncement
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic reversal that sets up the historical plot. Reagan's intensity makes it work.

jokes.text: You're lying! He's George Washington. He tends not to do that.
jokes.punchline: Pointing out Washington's reputation for honesty
jokes.explanation: Smart use of historical reputation. The understatement makes Washington's credibility a punchline.

jokes.text: Hi. I'm Jerry. I like movies.
jokes.punchline: Random character with completely inappropriate casual introduction
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing disruption. The banality against the dramatic moment is expertly placed.

jokes.text: Mr. Fry! Why are all these people standing around... When they should be partying?
jokes.setup: Lars appearing angry about the party
jokes.punchline: Reveals he wants them to party more, not less
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that subverts expectations about the stern boss character. Well-executed surprise.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, lbj, you want to get drunk? Oh, hell, yeah.
jokes.punchline: LBJ's enthusiastic acceptance
jokes.explanation: Plays on LBJ's known personality. The enthusiasm is in character and gets a chuckle.

jokes.text: Eh, not bad. A hint of vitalis and...
jokes.punchline: Treating it like wine tasting before the effect hits
jokes.explanation: Zoidberg treating poison like a sommelier is characteristically absurd. Sets up the time travel effect nicely.

jokes.text: I recognize this. It's the 1960s! I did a report on it for my drug-taking class.
jokes.punchline: Mentions having a 'drug-taking class'
jokes.explanation: Great absurd detail about future education. The casual mention of 'drug-taking class' is perfectly Futurama.

jokes.text: Hi, I'm Andy warhol, and you're some kind of marvelous lobster-man.
jokes.punchline: Warhol's artistic appreciation of the alien
jokes.explanation: Perfect Warhol characterization finding art in the bizarre. The phrase 'marvelous lobster-man' is spot-on.

jokes.text: Say, you got any real soup, or just this schlock?
jokes.setup: Zoidberg at Warhol's soup can art
jokes.punchline: Dismissing famous art as 'schlock' while looking for actual soup
jokes.explanation: Brilliant art joke - Zoidberg missing the artistic value and wanting practical soup. Perfect character blindness to culture combined with art history knowledge.

jokes.text: That head gave me a total head trip! Really? I'm gonna lick Herbert Hoover's head.
jokes.setup: Someone describing psychedelic experience as 'head trip'
jokes.punchline: Bender taking it literally with actual head licking
jokes.explanation: Good pun on 'head trip' that Bender would absolutely miss. The literal interpretation is very in character.

jokes.text: I'm not a still. I just smell like one.
jokes.setup: Someone mistaking Bender for alcohol equipment
jokes.punchline: Bender's matter-of-fact correction about his smell
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender self-awareness about his alcoholic nature. The casual admission is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Very observant, Professor. To preserve the heads, we use a rare form of powdered crystalline opal. This is the world's entire supply. Yet it's so powerful, it should last a thousand years.
jokes.setup: Explaining the precious opal supply will last forever
jokes.punchline: Sets up for it to be completely wasted on time travel
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the irony of wasting the precious resource. More of a plot setup than a standalone joke.

jokes.text: Because it didn't! Uh, yeah-huh.
jokes.setup: Professor explaining why they didn't have hallucinations
jokes.punchline: Immediate childish contradiction
jokes.explanation: Simple contradiction humor. Works but isn't particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: Ugh! Schlock and more schlock.
jokes.setup: Zoidberg back in present, looking at art again
jokes.punchline: Still dismissing the soup can art as worthless
jokes.explanation: Nice callback showing Zoidberg's consistent cultural blindness. The repetition reinforces the character trait.

jokes.text: I've discovered a new means of time travel. I'll call it the Farnsworth effect!
jokes.punchline: Naming it after himself despite the family shame
jokes.explanation: Professor's ego overriding the family shame is very in character. The enthusiasm despite recent humiliation works.

jokes.text: Professor, no! You can't tongue the father of our country!
jokes.setup: Professor about to lick Washington's head
jokes.punchline: Leela's horrified reaction using 'tongue' as verb
jokes.explanation: Great phrasing that makes the innocent act sound inappropriate. The formality of 'father of our country' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Rush hour.
jokes.punchline: Modern term applied to colonial era traffic
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective anachronism. Perfect economy - one phrase captures the visual gag completely.

jokes.text: Our nation's official joke state shall be new Jersey.
jokes.punchline: New Jersey as the official target of jokes
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke about New Jersey's reputation formalized by the founding fathers. The official tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Watch it, wiggy. I ain't no stinking crock. We'll see about that in five hours.
jokes.punchline: Bender's modern slang and Hamilton's crock pot timing
jokes.explanation: Bender's modern attitude clashing with colonial setting. Hamilton's practical response adds to the anachronism humor.

jokes.text: Uh... ever been to Peru? Certainly not. I'm from Peru.
jokes.setup: Leela asked about her origin by founding father
jokes.punchline: Awkward lie immediately exposed
jokes.explanation: Good awkward moment with Leela's bad lying. The immediate contradiction creates the cringe humor.

jokes.text: When he's not in Charlotte or Marybelle or Louisa.
jokes.punchline: Implying Franklin is with various women
jokes.explanation: Plays on Franklin's reputation as a ladies' man. Subtle enough to work in the historical context.

jokes.text: I'm sure no one's ever said this before, but I must get to Philadelphia as quickly as possible!
jokes.setup: Fry needing to get to Philadelphia urgently
jokes.punchline: Claiming no one's ever said this obvious thing before
jokes.explanation: Mild irony about the commonality of wanting to reach Philadelphia quickly. The setup phrase helps the joke.

jokes.text: Oh! Why couldn't my disgraced ancestor have lived in the nascar era?
jokes.setup: Professor lamenting the difficulty of colonial travel
jokes.punchline: Wishing ancestor lived when travel was easier via NASCAR
jokes.explanation: Amusing contrast between colonial era and modern racing. The complaint format works well for the Professor.

jokes.text: I ain't no stinking steam engine!
jokes.punchline: Repeats his earlier denial format with different machine
jokes.explanation: Decent callback that establishes Bender's running problem with being mistaken for period technology.

jokes.text: Mostly machine. Might be a couple of dead cats in there.
jokes.setup: Franklin asking if Bender is man or machine
jokes.punchline: Bender's casual admission about dead cats inside him
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender dark humor delivered casually. The uncertainty about dead cats is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: More like Taxachufetts. That's just how we print 's's, you ftupid fhitheads.
jokes.punchline: Franklin explaining colonial printing while demonstrating it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant historical linguistics joke that actually educates while being funny. Franklin's insult using the 'f' convention is perfect.

jokes.text: I call it the Franklinator.
jokes.punchline: Combining his name with 'Terminator' suffix
jokes.explanation: Solid anachronism joke. The '-inator' suffix is overused in comedy but works okay here with Franklin.

jokes.text: I call it the Franklinator
jokes.punchline: Using modern 'inator' naming convention in colonial times
jokes.explanation: Good anachronistic naming joke. The -inator suffix feels modern but fits Franklin's inventor persona.

jokes.text: Bender, do you know what a chamber pot is? I don't know, and I don't care.
jokes.setup: Leela asking if Bender understands what he's about to touch
jokes.punchline: Bender's willful ignorance before touching poop
jokes.explanation: Bender's characteristic indifference to consequences. Sets up the gross-out humor effectively.

jokes.text: Bender bit a poo penny. Bender bit a poo penny.
jokes.setup: Bender testing the coin's authenticity by biting it
jokes.punchline: Repeated emphasis on the disgusting action
jokes.explanation: Basic gross-out humor with repetition for emphasis. The alliteration helps but it's fairly crude humor.

jokes.text: There's only like 40 people who do anything around here.
jokes.punchline: Franklin's casual observation about colonial economy size
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-commentary on colonial society's small scale. The casual modern phrasing from Franklin works well.

jokes.text: and bender kicked our horse to death.
jokes.setup: Explaining why they can't travel by horse
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of Bender's violent solution
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender violence mentioned matter-of-factly. The casual tone makes the dark humor work.

jokes.text: Stupid Franklin. I will get you!
jokes.setup: Bender being fired from a cannon
jokes.punchline: His continued anger during flight
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy. Bender staying in character while being launched is mildly amusing.

jokes.text: I'm 40% scrap metal.
jokes.punchline: Bender's characteristic percentage fact
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender callback to his percentage claims. Perfect character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Teakettles. Yes, I've made some new teakettles. Perfect for special occasions. Like hanging king George in effigy...
jokes.punchline: Reveals they're for revolutionary activities
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing how colonial rebels had to disguise their activities. The innocent tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: What are you looking at, you British bastards? Paul, we talked about this.
jokes.setup: Revere being hostile to assumed British spies
jokes.punchline: Franklin's weary correction of Revere's behavior
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamic showing Revere's paranoia and Franklin's exhaustion with it. The 'we talked about this' is perfect.

jokes.text: Aw, I got a chipmunk.
jokes.punchline: Someone disappointed with their weapon choice
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor about getting an impractical 'weapon'. The disappointment sells it adequately.

jokes.text: I is Davy Farnsworth.
jokes.punchline: His poor grammar revealing lower class status
jokes.explanation: Simple characterization through grammar. Establishes him as uneducated but doesn't do much more.

jokes.text: You idiot. I'm not a Cannon.
jokes.setup: Farnsworth trying to fire Bender like a cannon
jokes.punchline: Bender's indignant correction
jokes.explanation: Bender's dignity being offended by being used as weaponry. The setup is more visual than verbal.

jokes.text: Huh. I guess I am a Cannon.
jokes.punchline: Immediate evidence to the contrary when he fires
jokes.explanation: Good immediate reversal joke. Bender's casual acceptance of being wrong is in character.

jokes.text: Huzzah! I've saved the Farnsworth name.
jokes.punchline: Sets up for them having broken history instead
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup for the consequences. The celebration before understanding the results is very Professor.

jokes.text: He has so much stuff. He won't miss this.
jokes.punchline: Casual theft justified by abundance
jokes.explanation: Sets up the historical consequence nicely. Fry's casual criminality is in character.

jokes.text: They had two burning, so I figured they wouldn't miss one.
jokes.setup: Explaining where he got the lantern
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's destroyed Paul Revere's signal system
jokes.explanation: Excellent historical irony. Fry's innocent logic destroying a crucial historical moment is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: The British are coming. The British are coming. By land. By land. Gotta treat this OCD. Gotta treat this OCD.
jokes.punchline: Gets stuck in repetitive loop, self-diagnoses OCD
jokes.explanation: Clever twist on the famous ride with modern psychological awareness. The repetition makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Fry, you dope. You've really screwed the grannie this time.
jokes.punchline: Bender's colorful euphemism for disaster
jokes.explanation: 'Screwed the grannie' is a nice Bender-esque euphemism. Characteristically crude but family-friendly.

jokes.text: I guess everything worked out jolly all right, eh, governor?
jokes.punchline: Speaking with British accent, revealing the change
jokes.explanation: Perfect reveal of the alternate timeline through accent change. The irony of 'worked out' when everything's wrong is excellent.

jokes.text: Appears we've changed history too much. Disaster of nightmarish proportions and whatnot.
jokes.punchline: Professor's understated British reaction
jokes.explanation: Good British understatement humor. The casual 'and whatnot' after describing disaster is perfect.

jokes.text: Who's for tea and shmavouries?
jokes.punchline: Made-up British-sounding word for snacks
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay creating fake British terminology. 'Shmavouries' sounds appropriately silly.

jokes.text: The revolution? 1776 and what all? Oh, the colonial dustup.
jokes.setup: Someone asking about the American Revolution
jokes.punchline: Dismissing it as a minor 'dustup'
jokes.explanation: Excellent British dismissiveness reducing the American Revolution to a minor inconvenience. 'Dustup' is the perfect word choice.

jokes.text: Turn on the tellyvisor, Dr. Zoidsmythe. I'll dial up the beeb. They've always got a nice docky-wocky on.
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top British slang for television and BBC
jokes.explanation: Good exaggerated British slang. 'Tellyvisor' and 'docky-wocky' are appropriately silly compound words.

jokes.text: all thanks to a nutter known as 'wrong-way revere.'
jokes.punchline: Paul Revere gets infamous nickname for going wrong direction
jokes.explanation: Perfect historical irony nickname. 'Wrong-way' Revere is a great callback to the earlier mistake.

jokes.text: But we came by sea, we did. Surprised the yanks at Lexington, ending the rebellion with nary a kipper scuffed.
jokes.setup: Explaining how they won the war
jokes.punchline: British idiom about easy victory using fish terminology
jokes.explanation: Good made-up British idiom. 'Nary a kipper scuffed' sounds authentically British while being absurd.

jokes.text: and bravely smothered him with his wig.
jokes.setup: Describing how David Farnsworth killed Washington
jokes.punchline: Absurd method of assassination using period-appropriate item
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist assassination method. Using a wig as murder weapon is perfectly period-appropriate and ridiculous.

jokes.text: Say, how is it that we've got socialized medicine, but me teeth still look like this?
jokes.punchline: Self-aware joke about British dental stereotype
jokes.explanation: Self-aware stereotype humor about British teeth. Gets points for acknowledging the cliché.

jokes.text: Don't want to. Rather nice, living under the crown and so forth.
jokes.setup: Asked about going back to fix timeline
jokes.punchline: Bender likes the British rule better
jokes.explanation: Bender's selfish preference for the alternate timeline is in character. The British phrasing helps.

jokes.text: C-consort? That's right, love. Fer centuries, the Farnsworth gentlemen has provided service to the queen.
jokes.setup: Professor learning about his role as consort
jokes.punchline: Queen's matter-of-fact explanation of sexual duties
jokes.explanation: Mild sexual humor with the Professor's discomfort. The historical twist on family 'service' works adequately.

jokes.text: Now, close your eyes and think of england.
jokes.punchline: Classic British phrase about enduring unpleasant duty
jokes.explanation: Classic British phrase used appropriately. The historical reference adds authenticity to the joke.

jokes.text: He crowned me in the knickers and nicked me crown.
jokes.setup: Queen explaining what the Professor did
jokes.punchline: British slang for hitting and stealing
jokes.explanation: Good British slang wordplay. 'Crowned in the knickers' and 'nicked me crown' have nice rhythm.

jokes.text: Or we'll be forced to continue chasing you, since we don't carry guns.
jokes.setup: British police trying to stop the Professor
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging their ineffective threat due to no weapons
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke about British police being unarmed. The logic of continuing an ineffective chase is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Hey, you're the plonkers what candlesticked me.
jokes.punchline: British slang for being hit with candlestick
jokes.explanation: Decent callback with British slang. 'Candlesticked' as a verb is mildly amusing.

jokes.text: and now I'm married to one of the spice girls. Don't rightly know which one.
jokes.setup: David explaining his success in alternate timeline
jokes.punchline: Married to Spice Girls member but doesn't know which
jokes.explanation: Good anachronism joke placing 1990s pop group in alternate timeline. The indifference about which one is funny.

jokes.text: Even John Tyler. That's the first time anyone's spoken to me in 1,200 years.
jokes.setup: All presidents being back in museum
jokes.punchline: Tyler's comment on his historical obscurity
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-joke about Tyler being a forgotten president. The precise timing of '1,200 years' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: from Chester A. Arthur to Chester Z. Arthur,
jokes.setup: Professor listing all the presidents present
jokes.punchline: Implying there were 26 Chester Arthurs
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd joke suggesting alphabetical progression of Chester Arthurs. Simple concept executed perfectly.
